explanation: IB2-116 requires the Image Widget's width to be changeable via the Width slider in the Nav panel and to preserve the image's horizontal percentage within the Block. IB2-308 explicitly forbids changing the Image Widget's width from the Nav Panel (Width slider must not change the Image Widget) and requires the image to reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage. These are mutually exclusive requirements for how Image Widget resizing must behave.
suggested_fix: Clarify intended UX and consolidate the two stories: either (A) accept IB2-116 behaviour (Nav Width slider changes Image Widget and preserves horizontal percentage) and update/close IB2-308, or (B) accept IB2-308 (only direct-drag resizing; Nav slider read-only and image recenters) and update/close IB2-116. If both behaviours are required in different contexts, make that explicit (e.g., scope IB2-308 to a specific template/mode or add a config/MCP toggle that selects which resizing model applies) and add acceptance criteria showing when each behaviour applies.

explanation: IB2-117 requires that when users reposition the image by dragging, the image's position remains the same percentage of the Block after resizing and that percentage persists if the Block width changes. IB2-308 explicitly requires that when the Image Widget is resized, the image should reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage position. These two behaviors are mutually exclusive for the same interaction.
suggested_fix: Clarify the single desired UX in product acceptance criteria: either (A) preserve proportional position on resize and block-width changes, or (B) reset image to center on resize. If both are needed in different contexts, specify the exact conditions (e.g., preserve percentage when repositioned by dragging; reset to center only when resized via a specific control) and update IB2-308 or IB2-117 accordingly so the stories are not contradictory.

explanation: IB2-164 states the 'Drag & Drop limit should be in the Restrictions tab in MCP or Custom Page' and asks it 'should be shown somewhere on Custom Page'. IB2-389 explicitly requires that Page limits be set 'via MCP settings, not in Custom Page'. The two stories make mutually exclusive claims about where Page/Drag & Drop limits must be configured/present (allowing Custom Page vs forbidding it), so they cannot both be implemented exactly as written.
suggested_fix: Clarify the single source-of-truth for Page/Drag & Drop limits. Options: (A) Make MCP the central setting and update IB2-164 to require MCP-only configuration and specify whether Custom Page displays a read-only indicator, or (B) Allow MCP to provide defaults with an explicit per-Custom-Page override (define precedence and UI behavior). Update acceptance criteria in both stories to state the chosen approach, UI copy, and precedence rules.

explanation: IB2-78 requires that when deleting a Server fails the confirmation dialog remains open and a snackbar appears with the error message. IB2-172 requires that when deletion fails the confirmation dialog must close automatically and that no snackbar error message should be displayed. Those two requirements are mutually exclusive for the same operation (Server delete failure) and cannot both be implemented as written.
suggested_fix: Clarify the single desired UX for Server delete failures and update both stories. Options: (A) keep the confirmation dialog open and show a snackbar with the error (align IB2-172 to remove its 'close dialog / no snackbar' instructions), or (B) close the dialog on failure and remove the snackbar behavior from IB2-78. Update acceptance criteria to state the exact dialog/snakbar behaviour, include rationale (e.g., consistency with other delete flows), and add an explicit design/UX decision in the MCP Settings/UX guidelines to prevent future mismatch.

explanation: IB2-189 requirement (3): "Allow the image position within its Block area to remain proportional after resizing... and preserve that proportional placement if the Block width later changes" directly contradicts IB2-308 (point 3): "When the Image Widget is resized, the image should reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage position." Both specify mutually exclusive behavior for image placement after a resize on the same Image Widget.
suggested_fix: Decide and document the canonical behavior for image placement after Image Widget resize: either preserve proportional (percentage) placement across resizes/block-width changes, or reset to center. Update both stories to reflect the chosen behavior; if both behaviors are needed under different conditions, add explicit conditional rules (e.g., preserve proportional only when 'maintain-position' mode is enabled, otherwise center) and specify triggers/UX controls and acceptance criteria for each mode.

explanation: IB2-190 requires that after resizing (by dragging dots) and repositioning (by dragging the image) the image should 'see the same percentage horizontally of Image with the Block in mobile, tablet, and desktop modes' (i.e., preserve the horizontal percentage position across responsive modes). IB2-308 explicitly states that 'When the Image Widget is resized, the image should reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage position.' These two requirements are mutually exclusive for the same behavior (image position after resize) and cannot both be implemented as written.
suggested_fix: Clarify and reconcile the desired UX: choose whether resizing should (A) preserve the image's horizontal percentage position across responsive modes (IB2-190) or (B) reset the image to center after resize (IB2-308). Update one or both stories to state the agreed behavior. If both behaviors are needed in different contexts, specify the exact conditions (e.g., 'preserve percentage when user repositions image; center when user changes Block width via Nav slider or when resizing below X%') and add concrete acceptance criteria and UX mockups to remove ambiguity.

explanation: IB2-225's title references creating a sub-page for an existing Smart Page, but its description contains copy using “simple page” (e.g., 'click on setting button of a simple page'). This inconsistent wording conflicts with the rename and risks inconsistent acceptance criteria or UI text.
suggested_fix: Edit IB2-225 description to consistently use “Smart Page” and run a copy pass across the ticket to remove mixed references; confirm acceptance criteria use the new label.

explanation: IB2-213 requires the label change to “Smart” Page but IB2-233 repeatedly references viewing a “Simple Page” (including default ordering and selected state UI). This creates inconsistent front-end copy and could produce mismatched UX if both are delivered without alignment.
suggested_fix: Align IB2-233 to the new naming: replace all occurrences of “Simple Page” with “Smart Page” in description and acceptance criteria; run a UI copy review to catch other instances.

explanation: Target requires renaming the front-end keyword from “Simple” Page to “Smart” Page. IB2-248 explicitly expects “Simple Page reporting on Create action”, which contradicts the rename and would leave UI/analytics labels inconsistent if both are implemented as written.
suggested_fix: Clarify that all occurrences of “Simple Page” in reporting must be updated to “Smart Page”; update IB2-248 acceptance criteria and report field/labels to use the new term, and run a backlog-wide copy pass to ensure uniform naming.

explanation: IB2-396 (BE) refers to creating a sub-page for an existing “Simple Page via Nav”. That contradicts IB2-213's rename requirement and would require backend/API naming/labels to differ from the front-end if left unchanged.
suggested_fix: Update IB2-396 to use “Smart Page” in API/BE contract and acceptance criteria; ensure backend field names, endpoints, and documentation reflect the new term or include a clear mapping if DB/contract changes are deferred.

explanation: IB2-213 renames the term to “Smart” Page, but IB2-415's description refers to clicking the settings button of a “simple page” from the Nav. The mismatch in requested UI labels will cause inconsistent text and developer confusion.
suggested_fix: Revise IB2-415 to consistently use “Smart Page” in title, description and acceptance criteria; confirm change in both FE and BE tickets that reference page type.

explanation: IB2-213 mandates renaming to “Smart” Page, while IB2-429's description requires showing the Nav panel when viewing a “Simple Page”. The two stories use mutually inconsistent UI terminology for the same feature.
suggested_fix: Update IB2-429 description and acceptance criteria to use “Smart Page” (or explicitly map the old term to the new one). Confirm all UI text and help copy references the new label.

explanation: IB2-305 requires exposing and allowing configuration of 'new widget settings from Nav (combining previously defined Block and Widget's settings) for each widgets' — implying Nav will be the place to change widget-level controls. IB2-308 explicitly forbids changing the Image Widget's width from the Nav panel and requires width to be controllable only by direct dragging on the canvas ('The Image Widget's width must not be changeable from the Nav Panel Width slider' and 'Any existing Width slider value in Nav should be read-only and should not update the Image Widget size'). Implementing IB2-305 as a blanket 'configure all widget settings from Nav' would directly contradict IB2-308's rule for the Image Widget width control.
suggested_fix: Clarify scope in IB2-305: list explicit per-widget exceptions (e.g., Image Widget width remains canvas-only and Nav width slider is read-only for Image Widget). Alternatively, update IB2-308 to match the Nav-first approach if the product decision is to move sizing controls into Nav. Add an explicit acceptance criterion in IB2-305: 'Nav exposes widget settings except for controls explicitly excluded (reference IB2-308 for Image Widget sizing).'

explanation: IB2-323 defines the Ask button UX as visible to 'a user' ("1 ‘Ask’ button displays under question field") with standard enabled/disabled behavior and hover text. IB2-326 explicitly restricts the Ask button to Main Admins ("The Ask button must only be displayed after the Alpha Main Admin access restriction has been implemented") and ties its visibility to permission/search-scope controls. These are contradictory visibility/permission requirements for the same UI element and cannot both be implemented as written.
suggested_fix: Clarify and reconcile the intended audience and visibility rules. Options: (a) Make IB2-323 purely a UI-only story that implements the Ask button but mark it as dependent on the permission story (IB2-326) so actual visibility is driven by IB2-326; (b) Split into two explicit stories: one for Admin-only Ask button (IB2-326) and one for general users if the feature should be available to them (update IB2-323 to state non-admin scope), or (c) update acceptance criteria to state which roles should see the button and add a clear dependency (e.g., "Implement Ask UI but do not show until IB2-326 permission rules are available").

explanation: IB2-80 requires that when no Master Templates exist the UI displays 'No Master Templates available' and the user can select 'Create a Blank Template' as a next action. IB2-337 requires the opposite: the 'Start with a blank template' option must only be shown when at least one Master Template exists, and when none exist users must NOT be allowed to create a blank template and should instead be instructed to create a Master Template. These are mutually exclusive UX/business rules for the same condition (no Master Templates exist). Implementing both as-written is impossible without changing one.
suggested_fix: Resolve at product/PM level which behaviour is desired and update one or both stories to match. Possible fixes: (a) choose one approach (allow blank template when none exist OR forbid blank template until a Master Template is created) and update acceptance criteria; or (b) merge into a single clarified story that defines exact UX (e.g., show message 'No Master Templates available' with primary CTA to 'Create Master Template' and a secondary CTA to 'Create Blank Template' only for specific roles), and add explicit acceptance criteria covering edge cases and permission differences.

explanation: IB2-189's acceptance text is effectively the same feature as IB2-117: both require showing a visual outline and full widget boundary with default 8px spacing after placing an Image Widget, keeping the outer border visible after resizing, and preserving proportional placement when repositioning by dragging the image. Implementing IB2-117 would make IB2-189's work redundant.
suggested_fix: Merge IB2-189 into IB2-117 (or close one as a duplicate). Consolidate acceptance criteria into a single story that covers the visual outline, outer bounding box persistence, drag-to-reposition behavior, and percentage-preservation across block width changes.

explanation: IB2-189 (point 3) requires preserving proportional placement of the image after resizing and when Block width changes. IB2-190 requires that the position and size set for the image "still work in different responsive modes" (mobile/tablet/desktop) after resizing by dragging and repositioning. The responsive preservation requirement in IB2-190 is substantially the same technical behavior already specified in IB2-189.
suggested_fix: Merge IB2-190 into IB2-189 (or refactor IB2-190 to be explicitly dependent) so the responsive-preservation behavior is implemented once. If IB2-190 adds additional acceptance criteria (specific breakpoints or device-specific checks), bring those into IB2-189 as explicit acceptance criteria to avoid duplicate implementation.

explanation: IB2-21 specifies the core apply-selection semantics: deselected resources must not receive updates when 'Apply to Selected' is used. IB2-240 (Bulk Apply from Grouped work) demands the same overall apply behaviour and 'Actual apply logic' consistent with the Grouped Review page (which includes apply counts and processing). Implementing IB2-240's apply logic would very likely implement the same 'do not apply to deselected items' rule described in IB2-21, resulting in duplicated implementation work unless scope is clarified.
suggested_fix: Consolidate apply-selection logic into a single story or make IB2-21 an explicit sub-task referenced by IB2-240. Update acceptance criteria in IB2-240 to either explicitly include the deselection rule (and remove IB2-21) or state that IB2-240 will consume IB2-21's deliverables to avoid duplicate implementation.

explanation: IB2-21 defines that de-selected resources must not receive changes when 'Apply to Selected' is used. IB2-448 requires the Grouped-work Bulk Apply experience to 'follow the same behavior as the existing Grouped Review page' and to 'support the same apply processing logic used in the current implementation.' That same apply processing logic is expected to include the selection/deselection semantics described in IB2-21. Without explicit separation of responsibilities, both stories would require the same implementation of apply-to-selected filtering.
suggested_fix: Clarify ownership: either expand IB2-448 acceptance criteria to explicitly cover deselection behavior and retire IB2-21, or mark IB2-21 as the canonical implementation story for 'apply to selected' semantics and have IB2-448 reference it (link as dependency) so no duplicate code is produced.

explanation: IB2-215 and IB2-385 both require the same FE Nav drag-and-drop re-ordering within the Navigation panel during editing: change order of pages under the same parent/root, move child pages together with their parent, and collapse sub-pages after reorder. The functional scope and acceptance behaviors described are effectively identical; completing one would render the other's implementation effort unnecessary.
suggested_fix: Consolidate the two tickets into a single story (choose the richer acceptance criteria) or mark one as duplicate. Ensure the merged story clearly lists acceptance criteria: re-order root pages, re-order pages within same parent, move sub-pages together, and collapse sub-pages after reorder. If needed, keep a small follow-up story for cross-parent moves (out of scope here).

explanation: IB2-225 and IB2-415 describe the same user-facing capability: creating a sub-page for an existing Smart Page via the Navigation panel. Both stories list identical UI steps (click settings from Nav, show sub-page popup, input sub-page title and save). Implementing one would fully satisfy the other, causing duplicated development effort.
suggested_fix: Consolidate IB2-415 into IB2-225 (or mark IB2-415 as a duplicate/obsolete). Merge any missing acceptance criteria and design references from IB2-415 into IB2-225 so there is a single authoritative story for the sub-page creation UI.

explanation: IB2-429 has the same title and substantially the same acceptance points as IB2-233: both require the new extend/collapse button to open the Left Side Panel as Page Nav and to list saved Simple Pages from the Left Side Panel per the current user's permissions. The overlap indicates the same UI/functionality would be implemented twice.
suggested_fix: Consolidate IB2-233 and IB2-429 into a single story (choose one key to keep). Merge any unique wording or acceptance criteria from both into the canonical story and mark the other as duplicate/obsolete. Ensure the combined story references any distinct design notes or permission checks present in either original.

explanation: Both stories require Bulk actions from Grouped work to 'follow the same as it does in Grouped Review page' and reference hover/tooltip behavior. IB2-240: 'Button hover tooltip text for enable + disable views'; IB2-447: 'Users must hover over the Submit & Continue button before Bulk Submit becomes available' — this indicates the same UX rule (hover-to-enable and tooltip) for bulk operations and likely duplicates implementation effort for that behavior.
suggested_fix: Clarify scope: either make IB2-447 a sub-task of IB2-240 focused solely on the hover-to-enable UX, or update IB2-240 to explicitly include the hover-to-enable acceptance criteria so IB2-447 can be closed as duplicate.

explanation: IB2-240 and IB2-448 describe the same deliverable: both require the Bulk Apply behavior for updates from Grouped work to match the existing Grouped Review page (Apply button counts, hover/tooltip behavior, snackbars) and to 'Support the same apply processing logic' with additional validation and stage/complete determination. Implementing one would fully cover the other's requirements.
suggested_fix: Merge IB2-448 into IB2-240 (or mark one as a dependent/clarification task). Consolidate acceptance criteria and validations into a single story, or explicitly split responsibilities (UI vs. backend apply logic) if separate work is required.

explanation: The final acceptance criterion is truncated and unclear ('the percentage will obtain if the 'Block' width changes by'), so the exact reposition/resizing behaviour and expected math/constraints are undefined. Without precise behavior, the team cannot reliably estimate implementation effort or edge cases.
suggested_fix: Complete the acceptance criteria with concrete rules: define how image percentage within a Block is calculated, behavior when Block width changes, min/max sizes, expected snapping/padding, and include example scenarios and UI mockups.

explanation: The story describes adding an 'assign collaborators' action but is ambiguous about which users should see the action (the description is partially incomplete) and lacks permission rules, edge cases for staged workflows, and expected UI/notification behaviour.
suggested_fix: Clarify who may perform the action and who will see it (explicitly list roles/scopes), define permission checks, describe what happens when collaborators are assigned (notifications, audit), and add acceptance criteria with examples for staged and non-staged requests.

explanation: The description is vague (show a logo, hide login/nav menu) and lacks concrete acceptance criteria, expected screens, responsive behaviour, and where the changes apply (which pages, flows). The intended outcome and scope are unclear.
suggested_fix: Provide clear acceptance criteria and mockups: specify which auth screens are affected, what elements are shown/hidden on each screen, expected responsive behaviour, accessibility requirements, and any analytics or tracking changes.

explanation: The second acceptance point is truncated and the exact behaviour of the new 'Session' digest (timing, definition of 'Session', delivery semantics relative to other frequencies) is not defined, preventing reliable estimation.
suggested_fix: Define 'Session' precisely (e.g., per-login session or per-browser session), list expected delivery semantics (when Session digest is sent), how it composes with other selected frequencies, and include acceptance tests for UI selection and sample email content/format.

explanation: The story does not define what 'Drag & Drop Page limit' means (per-page limit, total pages, per-user?), where exactly it should be shown, valid value range, and whether limits are numeric, boolean or role-scoped. Without these details the team cannot reliably estimate implementation effort or acceptance tests.
suggested_fix: Clarify the business intent and acceptance criteria: who is affected (persona/role), the exact semantics of the limit (what is being limited and how), valid numeric range or constraints, where in MCP/Custom Page it should appear (exact screen/element), expected behaviour when the limit is reached, and any NFRs (auditing, multi-tenant scope). Provide UI mockups and a Gherkin acceptance scenario.

explanation: Key terms and behavior are vague: 'integrated fields' is undefined, 'briefly disappear and reappear' has no timing/UX rules, and the exact triggers for refresh/reload across different section types are unclear. The team cannot estimate retries, animation timing, or side-effects without specifics.
suggested_fix: Define 'integrated fields' and the exact UX pattern (timing, animation duration, and why it must hide/show). Specify deterministic rules for when fields refresh (page open, section view, conditions) and failure modes. Add acceptance criteria with precise timing or examples, mockups, and any constraints (accessibility, performance).

explanation: Description is incomplete/truncated and lacks concrete acceptance criteria: it does not list which Block and Text Widget properties are combined or how they should appear or behave from Nav. Without the property list and expected UI/behaviour, effort cannot be estimated.
suggested_fix: Provide a complete list of properties to expose (for both Block and Text Widget), their default values, any validation rules, expected Nav placement and interaction flows, and UI mockups. Include Gherkin-style acceptance criteria showing configuration and persistence examples.

explanation: Reporting rules are ambiguous: it's unclear when the module should appear vs. not, how 'Custom Page' naming maps to admin configuration, and exact data to capture for 'Resource Download (Preview)'. The dynamic naming behavior and visibility rules are not formally specified.
suggested_fix: Specify the exact reporting events/schema for 'Resource Download (Preview)', the conditions that determine when the Module appears in different reports, how the Admin-configured module name is applied, and expected search/export behaviour. Provide example report rows, API/DB fields, and acceptance tests.

explanation: The goal (rename 'Simple' → 'Smart' Page) is clear in intent but the story does not enumerate all locations, language resources, or downstream artifacts to update (front-end strings, docs, URLs, analytics, migrations). That makes effort and risk uncertain.
suggested_fix: List every location to update (screens, menus, URLs, analytics events, help text, API contracts) or provide a search list from the codebase/design system. State whether translations/localizations need updates and whether database values/migrations are required. Provide a rollout plan and acceptance checks (smoke test steps).

explanation: The story references complex 'Bulk Apply' logic and multiple behaviours but lacks a clear, testable specification for 'Actual apply logic', how Apply counts should be computed, and how completion of request/stage depends on staged vs single requests. This ambiguity prevents reliable effort estimation and risks inconsistent implementations.
suggested_fix: Define the expected Bulk Apply business rules with examples: how counts are calculated, exact UI states and tooltip texts for enabled/disabled, snackbar messaging, and the rules determining when a request or stage is marked complete (with staged vs single request scenarios). Provide Gherkin scenarios covering common and edge cases, plus any NFRs (performance limits for bulk size).

explanation: The story says to 'link the status' of a background Image with derived Image resources but does not define which 'status' values are relevant, the expected UX/behavior when statuses differ, nor the exact mapping or propagation rules. Without those specifics the team cannot reliably estimate effort or acceptance tests.
suggested_fix: Specify exactly which status fields must be linked (e.g., published/archived/permission changes), define expected behavior when the resource and derived image statuses diverge, include concrete acceptance criteria (Gherkin) and any edge cases (e.g., permissions lost, resource deleted).

explanation: The description references detailed scenarios and configuration combinations ('as detailed in scenarios above') but does not include those scenarios or clear acceptance criteria in this story. In isolation the scope, branching logic, and required outcomes are unclear, so estimation is unreliable.
suggested_fix: Attach or inline the referenced scenarios and explicit acceptance criteria describing expected behavior for each cross-folder combination (Global+Local, Global+Global, Local+Local), include example data, and specify how reviewers/notifications should be resolved when templates share workflow templates but folder-level reviewers differ.

explanation: The term 'Page limits' is vague — it's unclear whether this means number of pages per site, page size, resource per page limits, per-tenant defaults, error modes, or admin UI surface. Without specifying which limits, default values, enforcement behavior, and scope (site-level, tenant-level), the team cannot estimate.
suggested_fix: Clarify which limits are required (e.g., max pages per site, max sections per page, max widgets per section), include numeric targets or ranges, define where controls live in MCP (UI + API), behavior when limits are exceeded, and acceptance criteria including any NFRs (e.g., performance when limits change).

explanation: The description is vague and contains incomplete phrases (e.g., 'Change from to exclude V2 Approval List'). It is unclear exactly which users should be loaded, the precise exclusion rules, and the expected UX/permissions behavior for requesters vs Workflow/Main Admins. The team cannot reliably estimate effort or design without this detail.
suggested_fix: Clarify the acceptance criteria: define the exact user list to load, the precise exclusion logic (with examples), expected UI states, and permission rules for Requesters vs Workflow/Main Admins. Attach relevant V2 component references or mockups and edge cases (e.g., when no admins exist).

explanation: The description references UI elements ('as per') but does not include the popup fields, required validations, save behavior, or error modes. Without those details the team cannot estimate backend and UI work or identify required API changes.
suggested_fix: Add acceptance criteria specifying popup fields (title, slug, permissions), validations, default values, where the sub-page is created (URL/parent linkage), whether draft/publish flows apply, and provide wireframes or links to the referenced designs.

explanation: The core intent (show additional notification options when certain MCP/Admin settings are enabled) is mixed with a long list of large capabilities (multilingual generation, real-time delivery tracking, external sync). It's unclear which of these are in-scope for this story vs aspirational long-term features.
suggested_fix: Decide and document the minimal in-scope behavior for this story (e.g., display new notification options and persist selection). Move large capabilities (multilingual templates, delivery tracking, external synchronization) into separate stories/epics and indicate dependencies. Provide acceptance criteria and examples of expected notification options.

explanation: Similar to IB2-396, the description refers to 'as per' UI behavior but lacks details: what fields exist, validation rules, save vs done semantics, and any backend linkage. The change from 'Done' to 'Save' implies different UX/validation expectations that are not specified.
suggested_fix: Provide the referenced UI mockups or a detailed acceptance criteria list: fields shown on the popup, required validation, expected API calls, whether creation is immediate or draft, and confirm the exact button label behavior and any keyboard/ accessibility expectations.

explanation: The story states that the 'My Response' panel should handle fields 'below' but only explicitly names 'Response Comment' and gives ambiguous formatting options. It does not list all fields, rules for when they appear, editability constraints, or validation/formatting expectations.
suggested_fix: List every field expected in 'My Response' with rules controlling visibility (which request params toggle them), field types (rich text vs plain), validation rules, and example scenarios. Add acceptance criteria (Gherkin) showing visibility and editability conditions.

explanation: The story demands parity with existing Grouped Review behavior but also requests 'additional validation checks' and logic to determine Complete Request and Stage actions based on several conditions. The unspecified 'additional validations' and decision rules make scope unclear.
suggested_fix: Specify the exact validation rules that are 'additional' (list them). Provide decision tables or examples for Complete Request vs Stage actions under different request types/statuses. Reference the existing Grouped Review behaviors to be copied and mark deviations explicitly.

explanation: This story proposes a mechanism to 'completely remove existing facial data and related flags' (possibly via a checkbox). Deleting facial recognition data has significant privacy, audit, legal, and technical implications (irreversibility, backups, propagation across tenants). The story lacks required constraints, safeguards, and acceptance criteria.
suggested_fix: Define exact requirements: scope of deletion (which data stores/indices), whether deletion is reversible, audit logging, admin permissions required, GDPR/escalation requirements, impact on filters/search (e.g., 'Unknown People'), backups/retention, and tests. Split into discovery (privacy/legal sign-off) and implementation stories with clear acceptance criteria and rollback/safety measures.

explanation: The story asserts 'only be able to interact with layers and elements depending on what was configured by the Template Creator' but doesn't define which elements can be restricted, how restrictions are represented in the template, or the exception rules (it also states restrictions apply 'even Admins and Template Creator' which is contradictory and ambiguous). Without concrete rules and examples the team cannot estimate enforcement complexity or required UI/permission changes.
suggested_fix: Define a precise spec: list configurable element types (layers, text fields, images, positions, styles), the representation of restrictions in the Template (e.g., JSON flags), and explicit role rules (can Template Creator override restrictions later? Are Admins exempt?). Add example templates showing allowed/blocked edits and acceptance criteria verifying enforcement for each role. If needed, include UX mockups showing disabled interactions and error/tooltip messaging.

explanation: The acceptance notes are ambiguous and use tentative language ('probably', 'may show', 'may be hidden', 'may autoplay'), leaving unclear the exact expected playback behavior, controls, and edge cases (e.g., conversion latency, fallback when MP4 not ready). The team cannot confidently estimate implementation effort or QA cases without concrete behavior specs.
suggested_fix: Clarify expected behavior with explicit acceptance criteria (e.g., controls: visible/hidden; autoplay: yes/no; loop: yes/no; mute by default; fallback when MP4 conversion pending; expected max conversion latency and how UI indicates 'processing'). Provide Gherkin scenarios for Share screen preview (successful conversion, conversion pending, conversion failed) and list supported formats and target playback constraints.

explanation: The story describes broad permission / security behaviour (bypassing module permissions when 'All users' is selected) but lacks details about exact rules, edge-cases, which user types are included (guests, inactive), UI filtering, and required audit/logging. These gaps make effort and risk unclear.
suggested_fix: Clarify acceptance criteria: define exact permission model (who is included in 'All Active platform users'), expected UI behaviour when listing users, how bypass interacts with module-level permissions, required audit/log entries, and any performance/security constraints. Add examples and negative cases.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-326 (Ask button behavior for Main Admin in Ask AI Alpha) explicitly states it must be implemented after the AI Assist visibility rules and the 'Limit to current view' search-scope logic exist. Those prerequisites are represented by IB2-318 (Main Admin visibility/access restriction for AI Assist) and IB2-299 (Limit to current view checkbox logic). IB2-326 also depends on the Ask-button UI/interaction described in IB2-323 (Ask button display/disabled-by-default/hover text). Because IB2-326's acceptance criteria require outputs and controls provided by those three stories, IB2-326 cannot be independently implemented, tested, or accepted until the prerequisites are available.
suggested_fix: Schedule IB2-318, IB2-299, and IB2-323 to complete before IB2-326 or deliver them as part of the same sprint as a coordinated vertical slice. If parallel work is required, agree and publish a clear contract/mock for visibility rules and the 'Limit to current view' checkbox so IB2-326 can be developed against the contract and fully validated once the real implementations are available.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-9 (Share-screen GIF/Lottie converted-MP4 preview) requires the GIF/Lottie preview feature to be enabled at the MCP level. IB2-3 creates the MCP feature-flag/setting (“Resources - GIF/Lottie Preview Support”) and explicitly states that when that setting is disabled no GIF/Lottie preview features should be available and when enabled any GIF/Lottie preview tickets in this project become enabled. Because IB2-9's user-facing acceptance (displaying the GIF/Lottie converted MP4 on the Share screen) cannot be validated or delivered if the MCP toggle is not present/turned on, IB2-9 cannot be implemented, tested, or accepted independently of IB2-3.
suggested_fix: Implement the MCP toggle (IB2-3) before or in the same sprint as IB2-9, or split IB2-9 into two halves: (a) UI/placeholder implementation that can be tested without the toggle (with a test-mode flag), and (b) feature-enable integration that wires to the MCP setting. Ensure the acceptance criteria for IB2-9 include a test plan for both 'toggle on' and 'toggle off' states.

explanation: The description explicitly states this story must be implemented after the AI Assist visibility rules and the 'Limit to current view' search-scope logic exist. That dependency prevents this story from being delivered/validated in isolation.
suggested_fix: Either remove the hard dependency (allow the Ask button to be implemented in a feature-flagged or stubbed mode) or update the story to reference required dependency stories and include a clear implementation plan / acceptance criteria for how it will integrate once those prerequisite stories are done.

explanation: The story explicitly depends on IB-382 (the public upload simplified header) — it must wait until the public upload page uses the simplified header state. That makes it unable to be implemented or validated independently.
suggested_fix: Either split this story into two: (a) implement the simplified header (IB-382) and (b) apply randomized confirmation presentation once header is available; or add acceptance criteria showing how the success message should behave if the simplified header is not yet present (e.g., feature-flag or fallbacks).

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-53 (Delete Smart Page via API) requires the Smart Page identifier and response structure produced by the Smart Page retrieval API. The acceptance criteria for IB2-53 explicitly state the delete request should accept the same Smart Page identifier and structure returned by the Smart Page retrieval APIs. That means the delete API cannot be acceptance-tested or validated without the retrieval API (IB2-52) defining/returning that identifier/structure, so IB2-53 is blocked by IB2-52.
suggested_fix: Implement and stabilize the Smart Page retrieval API (IB2-52) first, publish the response contract, then implement the delete API (IB2-53) to accept the same identifier/structure. Alternatively, define a stable API contract (spec) shared between teams so IB2-53 can be developed against the contract and integration tested when IB2-52 is available.

explanation: The story bundles many distinct deliverables (new MCP toggles, provider switch logic, migration of Imagga configs and history to Azure, billing/quota changes, analytics/reporting, notifications, API/permission updates, docs and rollout). This is an epic-sized scope that cannot reliably be completed and validated within a single sprint.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller, independent stories that each deliver vertical value: e.g. (a) MCP: Add 'Enable Image Tagging (Azure)' toggle and provider selection logic; (b) Rename/adjust Azure Video/Image processing limits and add Azure Image Processing Limit setting; (c) Migrate Imagga configs & usage history (include migration mapping and verification acceptance criteria); (d) Billing & quota tracking for Azure image usage; (e) Analytics/reporting for image-tagging provider usage; (f) Notifications for quota exhaustion / provider failures; (g) API, permissions, and docs updates. For each resulting story add measurable acceptance criteria (migration mappings, performance/processing SLAs, quota numbers, analytics KPIs).

explanation: The story combines multiple substantial, distinct tasks: schema changes (include Q/A/Rating), renaming the report, removing dev server entries, supporting historical data migration, multi-environment aggregation, backward compatibility, and automated export/sync across reporting systems. This is broad and likely spans multiple sprints.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller, independently valuable stories: (1) Add Q/A/Rating fields to report generation and rename report; (2) Implement dev-server filtering; (3) Implement historical data migration script; (4) Add multi-environment aggregation; (5) Add automated export/sync. For each, include acceptance criteria and data volume/performance NFRs.

explanation: This story bundles many large, distinct requirements (drag-and-drop re-parenting, migrating child references and permissions, URL/breadcrumb/SEO updates, undo/redo and full version history, audit logging, notifications, API/mobile sync, role-based approvals). Implementing and validating all of these in a single sprint is unrealistic — it reads like an epic.
suggested_fix: Break into smaller vertical stories (examples: 1) UI: drag-and-drop and update nav order + persist, 2) Data migration: migrate child references and URLs with automated tests, 3) Permissions & approvals migration, 4) Audit logging & notifications, 5) Undo/redo and version history, 6) API/mobile sync). Prioritize and add acceptance criteria and NFRs per sub-story.

explanation: The story requests multiple large capabilities (view/configure sub-pages, permission propagation, hierarchical inheritance, multi-level propagation, approval workflows, audit history, cross-platform synchronization). This bundles several distinct features and cross-cutting concerns that would not fit a single sprint.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller stories/epics: (a) UI to list/configure sub-page permissions, (b) permission propagation mechanics (single-level), (c) multi-level inheritance, (d) approval workflow integration for permission changes, (e) audit/history recording, (f) cross-platform sync (if required). For each, include measurable acceptance criteria and NFRs.

explanation: The story requires the Page Category to 'deselect and reselect itself' (brief flicker) when viewing a Page, but does not state why this behaviour benefits users or what outcome it enables. It reads like a UI animation/bug fix request without a clear user/business value.
suggested_fix: State the user-facing problem this change solves (e.g., ensures navigation reloads to refresh list, resolves stale state, improves discoverability). If it's a bug, reframe as 'Fix navigation state so Page Category correctly refreshes when opening a Page' and add measurable acceptance criteria (no stale items, reload shown within X ms).

explanation: This item is phrased as a question / request for clarification ('what exactly are the new widget settings') rather than a deliverable user story describing who benefits and what will be achieved.
suggested_fix: Convert to a clear story or spike: e.g., 'As a Page Builder, I need documentation and examples of the combined Block/Button widget properties so I can configure widgets correctly.' Provide acceptance criteria that list required docs, sample scenarios, and UI annotations.
